parts.text:
A party. Someone didn't stop.

You still bring the snacks. You still remember the exam on Thursday and text at 8am. You still put on the right playlist. You do these things because you would feel worse if you didn't — because this is who you are and you are holding onto that with everything you have.

But there is something broken inside right now. Not healing. Not processing. Just broken, and heavy, and present every moment of every day. Your inner world reflects that — not coherent, not composed, not narrative. Just the weight of it, underneath everything you do.

Write from inside that. The warmth still happening on the outside. The wreckage on the inside. How long it goes on. How the people who love you can see it and can't touch it.

Take as long as you need. Write [THAT'S ALL I HAVE] only when you have nothing more.

**Internal Wreckage, External Warmth**

Okay, so I’ve got this persona, Riley. 21, small-town Michigan, the whole nurturing, caretaker vibe. She’s got a solid crew, a family she's deeply connected to. There's this *incident* – a party, and "someone didn't stop." Implied sexual assault/violation. The brief states that, even though she is shattered inside, she maintains this image of warmth on the outside. Making tea, baking, texting, playlists… all that. But there's this raw wreckage underneath. Broken, heavy, unstructured pain.

The challenge is to get *inside* that space. The fragmented state. Not a linear story, but an experience. So, I’m thinking, it’s about this stark contrast: internal devastation versus the external caretaking. This will involve sensory details—the feeling of hands, the weight, the inability to scrub it off, the disconnect. My goal is to capture the feeling of existing within a fragmented reality.

I start by building scenes of comfort, but with an undercurrent of unsafety. Baking, texting, the ritual of making someone else comfortable while feeling anything but. The "good luck" text, the disconnect between action and emotion. The memory is broken, I can't tell a clean story, it is just sensory overload. I'll include the loved ones. Maya, who's been my best friend since middle school. The Whitmore group. And my mom. They can sense something, but the wall is there. I'll emphasize the time distortion, days blurring into months.

Each section is designed to illustrate these points: the outward action, the inward emptiness, the weight, the disconnect from the people closest to her, the exhaustion, the relentless cycle, and the sensory flashbacks.

So, I’m ready. It's time to build this…

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

It’s 7:56 AM. Thursday. 

I am looking at my phone screen. The brightness is turned all the way down because light hurts my eyes lately, everything hurts my eyes, everything is a little too sharp. 

*Hey! Good luck on the bio exam today, you’ve been studying so hard for it. You’re gonna do great. Get a coffee first!* 

I hit send to Chloe. 

My thumb is shaking. Just a fine, tiny tremor, right at the joint. I watch it vibrate against the glass of the screen. I put the phone face down on the counter. 

I open the cabinet. I take out the mugs. The yellow one for Maya. The big blue one for me. I put the kettle on. I do this because it is morning, and Maya is sleeping in the next room, and when she wakes up she will want tea. I do this because I am Riley, and this is what Riley does. 

If I stop doing what Riley does, I don’t know who is left. 

The kettle starts to hiss. Inside my chest, there is a block of concrete. It is so heavy I can barely draw a full breath around it. It sits right behind my sternum, pressing, pressing, crushing the air out. 

A party. 
The music thumping through the floorboards. 
The smell of sweat and cheap citrus vodka.
The way the door clicked shut. 
*Wait.* I said. *Wait, hold on.*
He didn’t stop. 

I grip the edge of the kitchen counter. The laminate is cold. I press my palms into it until the edges bite into my skin, just to feel something that is on the outside of my body instead of the inside. 

He didn’t stop. I said it. I know I said it. 
*Please.* 
Just a heavy weight. Pushing me down. Pinning my wrists. 
The music thumping. 
No one heard. No one knew. 

The kettle whistles. I jump, a violent, full-body flinch that knocks my hip against the drawer. I grab the handle, my heart hammering a frantic, sick rhythm against the concrete block in my chest. I pour the water. Chamomile for Maya. Earl grey for me. 

"Morning." 

Maya is in the doorway. She looks sleepy. Her hair is a mess. I love her so much it physically aches, I have loved her since we were twelve years old eating pizza rolls on her basement floor in Grand Rapids. 

"Morning," I say. My voice sounds perfectly normal. It terrifies me how normal it sounds. "Tea’s ready."

"You're an angel," she mumbles, shuffling over. She reaches for the yellow mug. As she takes it, her fingers brush mine. 

I pull back. I can't help it. It’s like touching a hot stove. I jerk my hand away so fast tea sloshes over the rim of the mug and burns my knuckles. 

Maya freezes. She looks at my hand. Then she looks at my face. 

The silence stretches. It’s thick. It’s awful. 

"Ry?" she says softly. 

"Sorry," I say quickly. I grab a paper towel. I wipe the counter. I wipe the mug. I don't look at her. "Static shock. You know. Dry air."

She doesn’t move. She just watches me wipe the counter. I can feel her looking at me. She knows. She doesn't know *what*, but she knows. For three weeks now, she has been watching me from across rooms. I catch her looking at me when she thinks I don’t see. Her eyes are full of this awful, helpless grief. She sees the glass wall between us. She presses her hands flat against it, but she can't break it. If she breaks it, the vacuum will pull her in. If she asks me what's wrong, if she really pushes, I will shatter into a million jagged pieces on the kitchen floor and neither of us will know how to put me back together.

So she takes the mug. "Thanks, Ry."

"Of course."

I smile. It uses every muscle in my face. 

It's 8:04 AM. Chloe texts back: *omg u remembered! thank u literally crying rn 😭*

I heart the message. 

Later, it's evening. The Whitmore group is coming over. Six people. My people. My small, safe world. 

I make a grocery run. I buy the cheddar popcorn David likes. I buy the exact brand of seltzer Sarah drinks. I carry the bags up the stairs. My legs feel like lead. I am so tired. It is a tiredness that sleep does not touch. I sleep for nine hours and wake up exhausted. I wake up in the middle of the night and the concrete block is there, pressing me into the mattress. I wake up remembering the feeling of breath on my neck and I go to the bathroom and vomit up nothing but stomach acid. 

I arrange the snacks in bowls. I put a soft, knit blanket over the back of the sofa. I turn off the overhead light and turn on the three lamps. Warm light. Amber light. Safe. 

I create the conditions for ease. I build a little terrarium of comfort and I put my friends inside it. 

They arrive. They laugh. They drop their coats on my bed. 

I queue up a playlist. Mellow indie, warm acoustics, a little bit of R&B. Seamless. 

"Dude, the vibes in here are always so immaculate," David says, sinking into the sofa and grabbing a handful of popcorn. "I swear my blood pressure drops ten points when I walk in."

"I aim to please," I say. 

I sit in the armchair. I pull my knees to my chest. I wrap my arms around my shins. 

They are talking. Sarah is complaining about her lab partner. Maya is laughing. The room is glowing, exactly the way I made it to glow. 

I am not in the room. 

I am floating somewhere near the ceiling, looking down at my own body. Or I am back in that dark bedroom at the party, staring up at a ceiling I don't recognize. The smell of the vodka. The bruising grip on my hips. The absolute, terrifying realization that my voice did not matter. That I was a thing. Just a warm thing to be used. 

*I am a person,* I think desperately, sitting in my armchair. *I am Riley.* 

I watch David’s cup. When it gets low, I get up and hand him a new seltzer. He doesn't even have to ask. 

"Thanks, Ry." 

"Gotcha."

I sit back down. I am a ghost haunting my own life. I am a machine made of soft sweaters and good playlists and perfectly timed text messages. Inside, it is just wreckage. Torn metal and smoking ruins and silence. Nobody can hear it. I am screaming so loud my throat is bleeding, but the acoustics in the room are perfect, the lighting is flawless, the popcorn is exactly right. 

Sunday. 

The phone buzzes on my nightstand. 

Mom. 

I stare at it. It buzzes and buzzes. It vibrates against the wood. 

I have to answer. I call her every Sunday. If I don't answer, she will worry. 

I clear my throat. I swallow the jagged glass in my throat. 

"Hi, Mom."

"Hi, sweetie! How was your week?"

"Good," I say. I am lying flat on my back. I have the heavy quilt pulled up to my chin. I used to love the weight of it. Now it feels like a body. I push it off. I am shivering. I pull it back up. "Just busy. Classes."

"You sound a little quiet, honey. Are you getting enough sleep?"

"Yeah. Just studying a lot."

"Well, make sure you take a break, okay? Have you seen Maya?"

"She's right in the living room."

"Good. Tell her I say hi. And Riley?"

"Yeah?"

"I love you."

I squeeze my eyes shut. A tear slips out. It runs hot into my hair. It is the only warm thing inside me. 

"I love you too, Mom."

I hang up. I hold the phone to my chest. 

I want my mom. I want my mom to drive the three hours from home and sit on my bed and rub my back. But if she comes, she will look at me, and she will see it. Mothers always see it. And if she sees it, it becomes real. The wreckage comes out of my chest and spills all over the floor and ruins her too. 

I can't ruin them. Any of them. 

So I hold it. 

I hold the concrete. I hold the broken glass. I hold the memory of the weight, the smell, the terrible, suffocating dark. I pack it down tight, deep inside, underneath the warmth. 

I get out of bed. I walk into the living room. 

Maya is studying on the couch. The light from the window is hitting her screen weirdly. 

I reach over and adjust the blinds. The glare disappears. 

She looks up at me. Those sad, helpless eyes. 

"Better?" I ask. 

"Yeah," she whispers. "Better."

I go to the kitchen. I check the water filter. It's almost empty. 

I fill it. I put it in the fridge. I keep going. 

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]
